title: torch.tensor
text: torch.tensor(data, *, dtype=None, device=None, requires_grad=False, pin_memory=False) constructs a new tensor by copying the data. The data argument can be a list, tuple, NumPy ndarray, scalar, or another tensor. Regardless of input type, torch.tensor always performs a deep copy, so the returned tensor does not share memory with the input. This makes it safe to modify the original data without affecting the tensor, but it also means it has a higher memory cost for large inputs. The dtype argument explicitly sets the data type of the returned tensor. If omitted, dtype is inferred from the data. The device argument places the tensor directly on the target device, for example device='cuda' or device=torch.device('cuda:0'), avoiding a separate .to(device) call. The requires_grad flag controls whether autograd should track operations on this tensor; it defaults to False. Because torch.tensor always copies, it should be avoided for existing tensors or large NumPy arrays when copying is unnecessary. For those cases, prefer torch.as_tensor, which may reuse the underlying data if dtype and device match, or torch.from_numpy, which shares memory with the NumPy array. Using torch.tensor on an existing tensor is a common source of unnecessary memory use and will emit a UserWarning suggesting .detach().clone() or torch.clone() as the clearer alternative. When constructing a tensor with a known target dtype and device, combining both arguments is the recommended idiom: torch.tensor([1.0, 2.0], dtype=torch.float32, device='cuda').

title: torch.as_tensor
text: torch.as_tensor(data, dtype=None, device=None) converts data into a tensor while avoiding a copy whenever possible. Unlike torch.tensor, which always makes a full copy of the input, torch.as_tensor will reuse the underlying memory when the input is already a tensor on the same device with a compatible dtype, or when the input is a NumPy ndarray on CPU. If the input is a tensor and the requested dtype matches the existing dtype, and the requested device matches the existing device, no copy is made and the same underlying storage is reused. If either the dtype or the device differs from the input, torch.as_tensor falls back to creating a new tensor, which does copy. For NumPy arrays, torch.as_tensor on CPU behaves similarly to torch.from_numpy: the returned tensor shares memory with the NumPy array, so changes to one are visible in the other. If a non-CPU device is requested, a copy is made to move the data. Use torch.as_tensor when you already have array-like data, especially an existing tensor or NumPy array, and you want to avoid the memory and time cost of an unnecessary copy. Use torch.tensor when you explicitly need an independent copy, or when the input is a plain Python list of numbers and you want the clearest constructor. The key semantic difference is that torch.tensor always copies, while torch.as_tensor may reuse existing data when dtype and device match.

title: torch.from_numpy
text: torch.from_numpy(ndarray) creates a tensor from a NumPy ndarray. The returned tensor shares the same underlying memory as the input NumPy array: modifying the tensor will modify the NumPy array, and modifying the NumPy array will modify the tensor. The tensor is always placed on the CPU, because NumPy arrays live in host memory. This shared-memory behavior is the most important distinction between torch.from_numpy and torch.tensor. torch.tensor given a NumPy array always makes a full copy of the data, producing an independent tensor. torch.from_numpy never copies, returning a tensor view that is bound to the lifetime of the NumPy array. The returned tensor inherits the dtype of the NumPy array. Supported dtypes include float64, float32, float16, complex64, complex128, int64, int32, int16, int8, uint8, and bool. If the NumPy array is read-only, the returned tensor can still be created, but writing to it is not safe and has undefined behavior, since the underlying storage is not meant to be modified. Resizing the NumPy array after calling torch.from_numpy can invalidate the tensor, so avoid resize operations on the source array. Use torch.from_numpy when you need a zero-copy bridge between NumPy and PyTorch, for example when passing preprocessed NumPy data into a model without duplicating memory. Use torch.tensor if you need an independent copy, or if you need the tensor on a non-CPU device. torch.from_numpy cannot directly place the tensor on cuda; you must call .to('cuda') afterwards, which then copies the data to the GPU.

title: torch.zeros
text: torch.zeros(*size, out=None, dtype=None, layout=torch.strided, device=None, requires_grad=False) returns a new tensor filled with the scalar value 0, with the specified shape. The size argument can be given as a sequence of integers like (2, 3) or as positional integers like torch.zeros(2, 3). The dtype argument sets the data type of the returned tensor. If omitted, it defaults to the global default dtype, which is torch.float32 unless changed via torch.set_default_dtype. The device argument places the new tensor directly on the target device, such as device='cuda', avoiding a later .to(device) call. The requires_grad flag sets whether the tensor participates in autograd tracking and defaults to False. torch.zeros always allocates new memory and does not inherit properties from any existing tensor. Use torch.zeros when you need a fresh zero-filled tensor of a known shape, independent of any input tensor. When you need a zero-filled tensor that inherits the shape, dtype, and device of an existing tensor, prefer torch.zeros_like, which automatically matches those attributes. Typical uses include initializing buffers, creating masks before filling, and building placeholder tensors in a training loop. For initialization of model parameters, prefer the torch.nn.init utilities rather than constructing zero tensors directly. The optional out argument can be used to write the zero-filled result into a pre-allocated tensor instead of returning a new one, which is useful for reinitializing an existing buffer without allocating fresh memory. The returned tensor is contiguous in memory by default. For a known shape, torch.zeros is preferred over constructions like torch.tensor([0.0] * n) because it avoids building an intermediate Python list and performs the zero-fill directly.

title: torch.zeros_like
text: torch.zeros_like(input, *, dtype=None, layout=None, device=None, requires_grad=False, memory_format=torch.preserve_format) returns a tensor filled with the scalar value 0, with the same shape, dtype, and device as the input tensor. This is the standard pattern for creating a zero-initialized tensor that matches another tensor's properties. The key difference from torch.zeros is that torch.zeros_like takes its shape, dtype, and device from an existing tensor, while torch.zeros requires all three to be specified explicitly. This makes torch.zeros_like ideal when you need a companion tensor whose shape, dtype, and device match a reference tensor, such as a model activation or a gradient buffer. Any of dtype, layout, or device can be overridden by passing them as keyword arguments. If omitted, each one is inherited from the input tensor. requires_grad defaults to False regardless of the input tensor's requires_grad value, so if you need gradient tracking on the new tensor you must set it explicitly. Use torch.zeros_like when you already have a reference tensor and want a zero-filled tensor that matches it. Use torch.zeros when you only have a shape, dtype, and device and no reference tensor. The like-variant is especially useful inside forward and backward paths and in training loops, where you often want to allocate a new tensor on the same device and dtype as an existing one without having to re-specify device='cuda' or dtype=torch.float32 each time.

title: torch.Tensor.backward
text: Tensor.backward(gradient=None, retain_graph=None, create_graph=False) computes the gradient of the current tensor with respect to graph leaves using the chain rule. If the tensor is non-scalar and requires gradient, the function additionally requires specifying a gradient argument, which should be a tensor of matching type and shape. This function accumulates gradients in the leaf tensors: each call to backward() adds new gradients to the existing .grad attribute instead of replacing it. Because of this accumulation behavior, you need to zero the .grad attributes or set them to None before calling backward() in each training iteration. If retain_graph is False (the default), the computation graph used to compute gradients will be freed after backward completes, so calling backward() a second time on the same graph will fail. Set retain_graph=True if you need to call backward() multiple times. If create_graph is True, the graph of the derivative will be constructed, allowing computation of higher-order derivatives.

title: torch.optim.Optimizer.zero_grad
text: Optimizer.zero_grad(set_to_none=True) resets the gradients of all parameters managed by the optimizer. This must be called before each training iteration to clear accumulated gradients from the previous step, because PyTorch accumulates gradients by default. The set_to_none parameter controls whether gradients are set to None or filled with zeros. Setting to None generally has a lower memory footprint and can modestly improve performance. However, it changes certain behaviors: a None gradient and a zero-filled gradient behave differently when the user accesses the .grad attribute and performs manual operations on it. After calling zero_grad(set_to_none=True) followed by a backward pass, the .grad attribute is guaranteed to be None for parameters that did not receive a gradient in that step. In a standard training loop, the sequence is: optimizer.zero_grad(), then loss.backward(), then optimizer.step().

title: torch.optim.Optimizer.step
text: Optimizer.step(closure=None) performs a single optimization step to update all parameters managed by the optimizer based on their current gradients. The closure argument is an optional callable that reevaluates the model and returns the loss; it is required by some optimizers like LBFGS but optional for most others including SGD and Adam. In a standard training loop, gradients are computed by calling loss.backward() before step() is called. step() then applies the optimizer's update rule to each parameter using the gradient stored in parameter.grad. For SGD, the update is param = param - lr * param.grad. For Adam, the update uses adaptive learning rates based on first and second moment estimates. After step(), the parameters have been updated but the gradients remain in .grad until explicitly cleared by the next call to optimizer.zero_grad(). To save and restore the optimizer state for resuming training later, use optimizer.state_dict() to serialize the state and optimizer.load_state_dict() to reload it. Both model.state_dict() and optimizer.state_dict() should be saved together in a training checkpoint.

title: nn.CrossEntropyLoss
text: torch.nn.CrossEntropyLoss combines nn.LogSoftmax and nn.NLLLoss in one single class. It expects raw unnormalized logits as input, not softmax probabilities. The input should have shape [N, C] where N is the batch size and C is the number of classes. The target should contain class indices as integers with shape [N], where each value is in the range [0, C-1]. CrossEntropyLoss usually expects integer class targets (class indices) instead of one-hot vectors because it internally uses the class index to select the correct log-probability, which is more memory-efficient and numerically stable than computing element-wise products with a one-hot vector. If you pass one-hot encoded targets, you must use the soft-label variant by passing them as float targets with the same shape as the input [N, C], which is supported in newer PyTorch versions but is not the standard usage. The loss function applies softmax internally, so do not apply softmax to the model output before passing it to CrossEntropyLoss. Doing so would apply softmax twice, leading to incorrect gradients and poor training. The weight parameter allows per-class weighting to handle class imbalance. The reduction parameter controls how the loss is aggregated: 'mean' averages over all elements, 'sum' sums them, and 'none' returns per-sample losses.

title: torch.optim.SGD
text: torch.optim.SGD implements stochastic gradient descent, optionally with momentum and weight decay. The core parameters are lr (learning rate), momentum (default 0, set to 0.9 for typical usage), and weight_decay (L2 regularization). In a standard training loop, the optimizer is used in three steps: first call optimizer.zero_grad() to clear gradients from the previous iteration, then call loss.backward() to compute new gradients, then call optimizer.step() to update parameters based on the current gradients. optimizer.zero_grad() sets all parameter gradients to zero or None, which is necessary because PyTorch accumulates gradients by default. If you skip zero_grad(), gradients from previous steps will be added to current gradients, leading to incorrect updates. optimizer.step() applies the update rule: for basic SGD without momentum, each parameter is updated as param = param - lr * param.grad. With momentum, the update maintains a velocity buffer that smooths gradient updates across steps. SGD with momentum is the most widely used optimizer for training convolutional networks and remains competitive with adaptive methods like Adam on many tasks. To save and restore optimizer state for resuming training, use optimizer.state_dict() and optimizer.load_state_dict().

title: torch.optim.Adam
text: torch.optim.Adam implements the Adam optimization algorithm, which maintains per-parameter adaptive learning rates using estimates of first and second moments of the gradients. The key parameters are lr (learning rate, default 1e-3), betas (coefficients for the moment estimates, default (0.9, 0.999)), eps (term added to the denominator for numerical stability, default 1e-8), and weight_decay (L2 regularization, default 0). Adam is used in the same training loop pattern as SGD: call optimizer.zero_grad() to clear accumulated gradients, call loss.backward() to compute new gradients, then call optimizer.step() to update parameters. The adaptive learning rate means Adam often converges faster than SGD in the early stages of training and requires less learning rate tuning. However, Adam uses more memory than SGD because it stores two state buffers (moment estimates) per parameter. To save and restore Adam state for resuming training, both model.state_dict() and optimizer.state_dict() must be saved, because Adam's moment estimates are part of the optimizer state. If you only save model weights and create a fresh Adam optimizer on resume, the moment estimates are lost and training effectively restarts from scratch.

title: torch.utils.checkpoint.checkpoint
text: torch.utils.checkpoint.checkpoint implements activation checkpointing (also called gradient checkpointing), a technique that trades compute for memory during training. Normally, PyTorch stores all intermediate activations during the forward pass so they are available for gradient computation during the backward pass. With checkpointing, intermediate activations are discarded during the forward pass and recomputed from saved inputs during the backward pass. This reduces peak memory usage at the cost of additional computation, so training will generally be slower. Checkpointing is most useful when training large models that do not fit in GPU memory without it, or when you want to increase the effective batch size without running out of memory. Usage: wrap a segment of the model's forward pass with torch.utils.checkpoint.checkpoint(function, *args), where function is a callable that runs the forward computation for that segment. The function will be called once during the forward pass with activations discarded, and called again during the backward pass to recompute them. For sequential models, torch.utils.checkpoint.checkpoint_sequential provides a convenience wrapper that splits the model into segments and checkpoints each one. Activation checkpointing is orthogonal to gradient accumulation. Checkpointing reduces memory per step by recomputing activations. Gradient accumulation simulates a larger effective batch size by calling backward() multiple times before calling optimizer.step(), accumulating gradients across micro-batches without increasing memory usage.

title: torch.cuda.amp.GradScaler
text: torch.cuda.amp.GradScaler helps prevent gradient underflow when training with mixed precision (float16). In mixed-precision training, some operations run in float16 for speed and memory savings, but float16 has a limited representable range and small gradient values can underflow to zero. GradScaler addresses this by scaling the loss before backward, which scales all gradients up, and then unscaling the gradients before the optimizer step, which brings them back to their true values. The typical mixed-precision training loop uses GradScaler together with torch.cuda.amp.autocast: run the forward pass inside an autocast context to automatically select float16 or float32 per operation, compute the loss, call scaler.scale(loss).backward() to compute scaled gradients, call scaler.step(optimizer) to unscale and apply the update, and call scaler.update() to adjust the scale factor for the next iteration. If the scaler detects inf or nan in the unscaled gradients (indicating the scale factor is too high), it skips the optimizer step and reduces the scale factor. This dynamic scaling ensures training stability despite the limited precision of float16. GradScaler is only needed for CUDA training with manual mixed precision; it is not needed when using float32 only or when using bfloat16 on hardware that supports it natively.

title: Tensor.requires_grad
text: Tensor.requires_grad is a boolean attribute that indicates whether the tensor is being tracked by autograd for gradient computation. When requires_grad is True, all operations on the tensor are recorded in a computation graph, and calling .backward() on a downstream result will compute gradients with respect to this tensor. When requires_grad is False, the tensor is not part of the computation graph and autograd will not compute gradients for it. By default, tensors created by user code have requires_grad=False. Tensors created as nn.Module parameters have requires_grad=True. To change requires_grad on an existing tensor, use tensor.requires_grad_(True) or tensor.requires_grad_(False). Setting requires_grad to False for specific parameters is the standard way to freeze parts of a model during fine-tuning. If you call loss.backward() and the computation graph does not include any tensor with requires_grad=True, autograd cannot compute gradients and will raise an error. This commonly happens when model parameters are accidentally detached from the graph, or when a tensor was created without requires_grad=True. Note that requires_grad=False does not freeze the entire model automatically. It only affects the specific tensor it is set on. To freeze all parameters of a module, you must iterate over model.parameters() and set requires_grad=False on each one individually.

title: Tensor.detach
text: Tensor.detach() returns a new tensor that shares the same underlying data but is detached from the computation graph. The detached tensor has requires_grad=False and will not have a grad_fn, even if the original tensor was part of a gradient computation. This is a per-tensor operation: it creates a new tensor object that is separated from autograd tracking while keeping the data. The key difference between detach() and torch.no_grad() is their scope and mechanism. detach() operates on a single tensor and removes it from the graph permanently. torch.no_grad() is a context manager that temporarily disables gradient tracking for all operations within its scope. After exiting the torch.no_grad() context, gradient tracking resumes for subsequent operations. Use detach() when you need a specific tensor's value without maintaining its connection to the computation graph, for example when storing intermediate activations for logging, or when passing a value to a loss function that should not receive gradients through that path. Use torch.no_grad() when you want to run an entire block of operations without gradient tracking, such as during model evaluation or inference. detach() does not copy the data. The detached tensor shares storage with the original. Modifying the detached tensor will affect the original tensor's data.

title: Tensor.grad
text: Tensor.grad stores the gradient computed by autograd for a leaf tensor with requires_grad=True. After calling loss.backward(), the .grad attribute of each leaf tensor that participated in the computation will contain the gradient of the loss with respect to that tensor. Gradients in PyTorch accumulate by default. Each call to loss.backward() adds the newly computed gradient to the existing .grad value instead of replacing it. This means that if you call backward() multiple times without clearing gradients, the .grad attribute will contain the sum of all computed gradients. This accumulation behavior is intentional and useful for gradient accumulation across micro-batches, but it requires explicitly zeroing gradients before each standard training step. To clear accumulated gradients, call optimizer.zero_grad() at the beginning of each training iteration, before computing the forward pass and calling backward(). Without zeroing, gradients from previous steps will contaminate the current step's update, leading to incorrect parameter updates. After backward(), only leaf tensors (those created directly by the user, not as a result of operations) will have their .grad populated. Intermediate tensors do not retain gradients by default unless retain_grad() was called on them before the backward pass.

title: torch.utils.data.Dataset
text: torch.utils.data.Dataset is the base class for all map-style datasets in PyTorch. A custom map-style dataset must implement two methods: __len__, which returns the total number of samples, and __getitem__, which retrieves a single sample by index. For example, a simple dataset might store data in a list and return individual items in __getitem__. The DataLoader uses __len__ to determine the dataset size for batching and shuffling, and calls __getitem__ to fetch each sample during iteration. Subclassing Dataset is the standard way to wrap custom data sources such as CSV files, image directories, or database queries into a format that DataLoader can consume. For simple cases where the data is already in tensor form, torch.utils.data.TensorDataset provides a ready-made Dataset that wraps one or more tensors and indexes them along the first dimension. For iterable data sources like streams or very large files that cannot be randomly accessed, use torch.utils.data.IterableDataset instead, which requires implementing __iter__ rather than __len__ and __getitem__. When designing a custom Dataset, keep __getitem__ lightweight: load or transform data on the fly rather than preloading everything into memory, especially for large datasets.

title: torch.utils.data.DataLoader
text: torch.utils.data.DataLoader wraps a Dataset and provides batching, shuffling, parallel loading, and other iteration utilities. Key parameters include batch_size (number of samples per batch), shuffle (whether to randomize sample order each epoch), num_workers (number of subprocesses for parallel data loading), collate_fn (function to merge a list of samples into a batch), and pin_memory (whether to copy tensors into pinned host memory before transferring to GPU). For training, set shuffle=True so the model sees samples in a different order each epoch, which improves generalization. For validation and test sets, keep shuffle=False so results are reproducible and unshuffled, making it easier to compare runs and debug. The pin_memory option allocates tensors in page-locked host memory, which enables faster asynchronous transfer from CPU to CUDA devices. It does not move tensors to GPU by itself; you still need to call .to('cuda') on each batch. pin_memory is most useful when training on cuda because it reduces the overhead of the CPU-to-GPU data transfer. When num_workers > 0, DataLoader uses multiprocessing to load data in parallel, which can significantly speed up I/O-bound pipelines. The drop_last parameter controls whether to drop the last incomplete batch if the dataset size is not divisible by batch_size.

title: torch.utils.data.default_collate
text: torch.utils.data.default_collate is the default collate function used by DataLoader to merge a list of individual samples into a single batch. It handles common types automatically: tensors are stacked along a new first dimension, NumPy arrays are converted to tensors and stacked, Python numbers are collected into a tensor, and strings are collected into a list. For datasets where every sample has the same shape and type, default_collate works out of the box with no custom code needed. However, default_collate fails when samples have variable length or incompatible shapes, because it cannot stack tensors of different sizes. In these cases you need a custom collate_fn. Common scenarios that require a custom collate_fn include: sequences of different lengths that need padding, samples that contain mixed types or nested structures, and batches where some fields should be collated differently than others. A custom collate_fn receives a list of samples from __getitem__ and must return a single batch structure. For variable-length sequences, a typical pattern is to pad all sequences to the length of the longest one in the batch and return both the padded tensor and a lengths tensor. Pass the custom function to DataLoader via the collate_fn parameter: DataLoader(dataset, collate_fn=my_collate_fn).

title: torch.utils.data.TensorDataset
text: torch.utils.data.TensorDataset wraps one or more tensors into a Dataset. Each tensor must have the same size in the first dimension. Indexing into a TensorDataset returns a tuple of slices from each tensor at that index. For example, TensorDataset(features, labels) creates a dataset where dataset[i] returns (features[i], labels[i]). TensorDataset is useful when your data is already loaded into memory as tensors and you do not need any custom loading or transformation logic. It provides __len__ and __getitem__ automatically, so it can be directly passed to a DataLoader. For more complex data pipelines that involve on-the-fly transformations, file I/O, or non-tensor data types, subclass torch.utils.data.Dataset instead and implement custom __len__ and __getitem__ methods.

title: torch.utils.data.WeightedRandomSampler
text: torch.utils.data.WeightedRandomSampler samples elements from a dataset with given probabilities (weights). Each sample is assigned a weight, and the probability of drawing a sample is proportional to its weight. This is commonly used to handle class imbalance: assign higher weights to underrepresented classes so they are sampled more frequently during training. The sampler is passed to DataLoader via the sampler parameter: DataLoader(dataset, sampler=sampler). When a sampler is provided, the shuffle parameter of DataLoader must not be set to True, because the sampler controls the iteration order. The num_samples parameter specifies how many samples to draw in one epoch; if set to the dataset length, each epoch draws the same number of total samples but with a different class distribution than uniform random. The replacement parameter controls whether samples can be drawn more than once in a single epoch. WeightedRandomSampler is one of several built-in samplers; others include RandomSampler, SequentialSampler, and SubsetRandomSampler.

title: Tensor.view
text: Tensor.view(*shape) returns a new tensor with the same data but a different shape. The returned tensor shares the same underlying data as the original tensor, so it is a view and no data is copied. Changes to the view will be reflected in the original tensor, and vice versa. The new shape must be compatible with the original tensor's size and stride layout. Specifically, view requires the tensor to be contiguous in memory. If the tensor is not contiguous, for example after a permute() or transpose() operation, calling view will raise a RuntimeError. In that case, call contiguous() first to make a contiguous copy, or use reshape() which handles both contiguous and non-contiguous cases automatically. The shape argument can include one dimension set to -1, in which case that dimension is inferred from the remaining dimensions and the total number of elements. For example, if x has shape [2, 3, 4], then x.view(2, 12) returns a tensor of shape [2, 12], and x.view(-1, 4) returns a tensor of shape [6, 4]. Because view never copies data, it is efficient for reshaping, but the contiguity requirement means it is not always usable. The key difference from reshape is that view is strict about contiguity and guarantees no copy, while reshape may return either a view or a copy depending on whether the input is contiguous.

title: Tensor.reshape
text: Tensor.reshape(*shape) returns a tensor with the same data and number of elements but a different shape. When possible, the returned tensor will be a view of the input, meaning no data is copied and the tensors share the same underlying storage. When the input tensor is not contiguous and the requested shape is not compatible with the existing strides, reshape will return a new contiguous tensor with the desired shape, which involves copying the data. This makes reshape safer and more flexible than view, which strictly requires the tensor to be contiguous and always raises an error on non-contiguous inputs. Like view, reshape supports one -1 dimension that is automatically inferred. For example, if x has shape [2, 3, 4], then x.reshape(6, 4) returns shape [6, 4], and x.reshape(-1) returns shape [24]. A practical guideline: use reshape when you are not sure whether the tensor is contiguous, because it will do the right thing automatically. Use view when you specifically want to guarantee that no copy is made and want a clear error if the tensor is not contiguous. After operations like permute() and transpose(), tensors are typically non-contiguous, so reshape is the safer choice in those situations. The returned tensor may or may not share storage with the original; do not rely on view semantics from reshape unless you have verified contiguity.

title: Tensor.unsqueeze
text: Tensor.unsqueeze(dim) returns a new tensor with a dimension of size 1 inserted at the specified position. The returned tensor shares the same underlying data with the input tensor. The dim argument specifies where the new dimension is inserted. A dim value of 0 inserts the new dimension at the front, and a value equal to the current number of dimensions appends it at the end. Negative values are also supported: dim=-1 inserts before the last dimension. For example, if x has shape [2, 3, 4], then x.unsqueeze(0) returns shape [1, 2, 3, 4], x.unsqueeze(1) returns shape [2, 1, 3, 4], x.unsqueeze(2) returns shape [2, 3, 1, 4], and x.unsqueeze(3) or x.unsqueeze(-1) returns shape [2, 3, 4, 1]. The total number of elements does not change; only the rank increases by 1. unsqueeze is commonly used to add a batch dimension or a channel dimension before passing a tensor into a module that expects a higher-rank input. The inverse operation is squeeze, which removes dimensions of size 1. unsqueeze is a view operation and does not copy data.

title: Tensor.flatten
text: Tensor.flatten(start_dim=0, end_dim=-1) returns a tensor with the dimensions from start_dim to end_dim inclusive collapsed into a single dimension. By default, start_dim=0 and end_dim=-1, which flattens the entire tensor into a 1D tensor. For example, if x has shape [3, 2, 4, 5], then x.flatten() returns shape [120], x.flatten(start_dim=1) returns shape [3, 40], x.flatten(start_dim=2) returns shape [3, 2, 20], and x.flatten(start_dim=1, end_dim=2) returns shape [3, 8, 5]. The product of the flattened dimensions becomes the size of the new single dimension. Dimensions outside the start_dim to end_dim range are preserved unchanged. flatten is equivalent to calling reshape with the appropriate shape, but provides a clearer intent when the goal is to merge adjacent dimensions. If the input tensor is contiguous over the flattened dimensions, the result is a view and no data is copied. Otherwise, a contiguous copy is made. flatten is commonly used before a fully connected layer in a neural network, where spatial dimensions need to be collapsed into a single feature dimension. For instance, after a convolutional layer producing shape [batch, channels, height, width], calling flatten(start_dim=1) produces shape [batch, channels * height * width].

title: torch.cat
text: torch.cat(tensors, dim=0) concatenates the given sequence of tensors along an existing dimension. All tensors must have the same shape in every dimension except the concatenation dimension. The result has the same number of dimensions as the inputs. For example, if a has shape [2, 3] and b has shape [4, 3], then torch.cat([a, b], dim=0) returns a tensor of shape [6, 3]. If a and b both have shape [2, 3], then torch.cat([a, b], dim=1) returns shape [2, 6]. torch.cat does not create a new dimension; it joins tensors along a dimension that already exists. If the tensors have different sizes in any non-concatenated dimension, torch.cat raises a RuntimeError. For example, concatenating a tensor of shape [2, 3] and a tensor of shape [2, 4] along dim=0 will fail because the second dimension, 3 versus 4, does not match. The key difference from torch.stack is that cat operates on an existing dimension and does not change the tensor rank, while stack creates a new dimension and increases the rank by 1. torch.cat always allocates a new tensor and copies the data from all inputs. It does not return a view.

title: torch.stack
text: torch.stack(tensors, dim=0) stacks a sequence of tensors along a new dimension. All input tensors must have exactly the same shape. The result has one more dimension than the inputs, with the new dimension having size equal to the number of input tensors. The dim argument specifies where the new dimension is inserted. For example, if a, b, and c all have shape [4, 5], then torch.stack([a, b, c], dim=0) returns shape [3, 4, 5], torch.stack([a, b, c], dim=1) returns shape [4, 3, 5], and torch.stack([a, b, c], dim=2) returns shape [4, 5, 3]. The key difference from torch.cat is that stack creates a new dimension and increases the tensor rank by 1, while cat concatenates along an existing dimension and preserves the rank. Because all input tensors must have the same shape for stack, it is stricter than cat, which only requires matching sizes in non-concatenated dimensions. torch.stack is equivalent to calling torch.unsqueeze on each tensor at the target dimension and then torch.cat along that dimension. torch.stack always allocates a new tensor and copies data from all inputs. It does not return a view. Common uses include combining a list of per-sample tensors into a batched tensor, or assembling time steps into a sequence tensor.

title: Tensor.permute
text: Tensor.permute(*dims) returns a view of the original tensor with its dimensions reordered according to dims. The returned tensor shares the same underlying data as the original tensor, so no data is copied. However, the resulting tensor is typically non-contiguous in memory because permute only changes the stride layout, not the data. As a result, calling view on a permuted tensor often raises a RuntimeError about non-contiguous memory. To reshape a permuted tensor, call contiguous() first and then view, or use reshape which handles non-contiguous inputs. For example, if x has shape [2, 3, 4], x.permute(2, 0, 1) returns a tensor of shape [4, 2, 3] that shares memory with x.

title: Tensor.dtype
text: Every torch.Tensor has a dtype attribute that indicates its data type. Access it with tensor.dtype, which returns a torch.dtype object such as torch.float32, torch.float64, torch.int64, torch.bool, and others. To check whether a tensor has a specific dtype, compare directly: tensor.dtype == torch.float32. The dtype determines the precision and range of values the tensor can hold. torch.float32 is the default dtype for floating-point tensors created by most factory functions like torch.zeros, torch.ones, and torch.randn. torch.float64 provides higher precision but uses twice the memory. torch.float16 and torch.bfloat16 are commonly used for mixed-precision training to reduce memory and increase throughput on supported hardware. Integer types include torch.int64, torch.int32, torch.int16, and torch.int8. To change a tensor's dtype, use tensor.to(dtype=torch.float16) or tensor.float(), tensor.half(), tensor.int(), and similar convenience methods. Note that changing dtype always produces a new tensor with copied data; it is not an in-place operation on the storage. When performing operations between tensors, PyTorch requires compatible dtypes in many cases. For example, torch.matmul requires both inputs to have the same dtype. If they differ, you must explicitly cast one tensor before the operation.

title: Tensor.to
text: Tensor.to(*args, **kwargs) performs dtype and device conversion on a tensor. It can be called in several forms: tensor.to(dtype), tensor.to(device), tensor.to(device, dtype), or tensor.to(other_tensor) to match another tensor's dtype and device. For example, tensor.to(torch.float16) changes the dtype, tensor.to('cuda') moves to GPU, and tensor.to('cuda', torch.float16) does both in a single call. If the tensor already has the requested dtype and is already on the requested device, .to() returns the same tensor without copying. Otherwise, a new tensor is created with the converted data. PyTorch does not automatically move tensors between devices. If one tensor is on cpu and another is on cuda, any operation between them will fail with a device mismatch error. You must explicitly move tensors to the same device before operating on them. The typical pattern is tensor_b = tensor_b.to(tensor_a.device) or tensor = tensor.to('cuda'). Similarly, PyTorch does not automatically cast dtypes for most operations. If you attempt torch.matmul on a float32 tensor and a float16 tensor, it will raise a RuntimeError about dtype mismatch. You must cast one operand first: a.to(b.dtype) or b.to(a.dtype). The .to() method is the unified way to handle both device and dtype conversion.

title: torch.float32
text: torch.float32 is the default floating-point dtype in PyTorch, also accessible as torch.float. It uses 32 bits per element and provides a good balance between precision and memory. Most tensor factory functions such as torch.zeros, torch.ones, torch.randn, and torch.tensor with float inputs produce torch.float32 tensors by default. To create a tensor with a different dtype, pass the dtype argument explicitly: torch.zeros(3, dtype=torch.float16). To check if a tensor uses float32, compare tensor.dtype == torch.float32. torch.float16, also known as half precision, uses 16 bits and is widely used in mixed-precision training with torch.cuda.amp. torch.bfloat16 is another 16-bit format with a larger exponent range, supported on newer GPUs and TPUs. torch.float64, also known as double precision, uses 64 bits and is used when higher numerical precision is required, such as in scientific computing. When mixing dtypes in operations, PyTorch generally does not auto-promote. For example, adding a torch.float32 tensor to a torch.float64 tensor may work with promotion, but torch.matmul between torch.float32 and torch.float16 will raise an error. Always verify dtype compatibility before performing operations.

title: torch.float16
text: torch.float16 is a 16-bit floating-point dtype, also accessible as torch.half. It uses half the memory of torch.float32 and is significantly faster on GPUs that support native half-precision arithmetic, such as NVIDIA GPUs with Tensor Cores. torch.float16 is the primary dtype used in mixed-precision training workflows, typically via torch.cuda.amp.autocast, which automatically runs eligible operations in float16 while keeping others in float32 for numerical stability. To convert a tensor to float16, use tensor.to(torch.float16) or tensor.half(). To move a tensor to cuda and convert to float16 in one call, use tensor.to('cuda', torch.float16) or equivalently tensor.to(device='cuda', dtype=torch.float16). The reduced precision of float16 means it has a smaller representable range and lower decimal accuracy compared to float32. Values outside the representable range will overflow to infinity or underflow to zero. For this reason, loss scaling is often used alongside float16 training to prevent gradient underflow. When performing operations, both operands generally must have the same dtype. torch.matmul between a float32 tensor and a float16 tensor will raise a dtype mismatch error. Cast one operand first using .to(dtype) before the operation.

title: torch.matmul
text: torch.matmul(input, other) performs matrix multiplication between two tensors. The behavior depends on the dimensionality of the inputs: for two 1D tensors it computes the dot product, for two 2D tensors it computes the standard matrix product, and for higher-dimensional tensors it computes batched matrix products with broadcasting. Both input tensors must have the same dtype. If one tensor is torch.float32 and the other is torch.float16, torch.matmul will raise a RuntimeError indicating that the dtypes do not match. PyTorch does not automatically cast operands to a common dtype for matmul. You must explicitly convert one operand before calling matmul: either a = a.to(b.dtype) or b = b.to(a.dtype). Both tensors must also be on the same device. If one is on cpu and the other is on cuda, matmul will fail with a device mismatch error. Move tensors to the same device first using .to(device). For 2D inputs with shapes [m, k] and [k, n], the result has shape [m, n]. For batched inputs, the batch dimensions are broadcast following standard broadcasting rules, and the last two dimensions follow matrix multiplication rules: [..., m, k] multiplied by [..., k, n] produces [..., m, n]. torch.mm is a strict 2D-only variant that does not support broadcasting. Use torch.matmul for general-purpose matrix multiplication.

title: Tensor.item
text: Tensor.item() returns the value of a tensor as a standard Python number. This method only works when the tensor has exactly one element. If the tensor has more than one element, .item() raises an error because the tensor cannot be converted to a single scalar value. For example, if x = torch.tensor(3.14), then x.item() returns the Python float 3.14. If x = torch.tensor([1.0, 2.0]), then x.item() raises an error because the tensor has two elements. This method is commonly used to extract scalar loss values for logging during training: loss_value = loss.item(). It converts the single-element tensor to a Python scalar, which is useful for printing, storing in lists, or passing to non-PyTorch code that expects a plain number rather than a tensor. For tensors with more than one element, use .tolist() to convert the entire tensor to a nested Python list, or index the tensor to select a specific element before calling .item(). For example, x[0].item() extracts the first element as a scalar. The returned type matches the tensor's dtype: a float32 tensor returns a Python float, an int64 tensor returns a Python int, and a bool tensor returns a Python bool.

title: torch.argmax
text: torch.argmax(input, dim=None, keepdim=False) returns the indices of the maximum values along a dimension. It does not return the maximum values themselves, only their positions. For a 2D tensor, torch.argmax(x, dim=1) returns a 1D tensor of integer indices, one per row, indicating the column position of the largest element in each row. When called without a dim argument, torch.argmax(x) returns the index of the overall maximum in the flattened tensor as a scalar tensor. The output dtype is always torch.int64 regardless of the input dtype. If you need the maximum values instead of indices, torch.argmax is the wrong choice. Use torch.max(input, dim) instead, which returns both the maximum values and their indices as a named tuple. The distinction is important: indices tell you where the maximum is, values tell you what the maximum is. torch.argmax is commonly used in classification tasks to convert model logits into predicted class labels: predicted_classes = torch.argmax(logits, dim=1). It is also used to find the position of the best score in attention mechanisms or beam search. For cases where multiple elements share the maximum value, argmax returns the index of the first occurrence.

title: torch.max
text: torch.max has two main calling conventions. When called with just a tensor, torch.max(input) returns the single maximum value across all elements as a scalar tensor. When called with a tensor and a dimension, torch.max(input, dim) returns a named tuple of (values, indices), where values contains the maximum values along that dimension and indices contains the positions of those maximum values. For example, values, indices = torch.max(x, dim=1) gives both the actual maximum values per row and the column positions where they occur. This is the key difference from torch.argmax, which only returns indices without the values. Use torch.max when you need the actual maximum values, and use torch.argmax when you only need the positions. The values tensor has the same dtype as the input. The indices tensor has dtype torch.int64. When keepdim=True is specified, the output tensors retain the reduced dimension with size 1, which is useful for broadcasting in subsequent operations. torch.max can also compare two tensors element-wise: torch.max(a, b) returns the element-wise maximum, similar to torch.maximum. For reduction operations, torch.amax is an alternative that supports reducing over multiple dimensions at once.

title: Module.train
text: Module.train(mode=True) sets the module to training mode. This method affects only certain layers that have different behavior during training and evaluation, specifically dropout and batch normalization. In training mode, dropout layers randomly zero out a fraction of input elements at each forward pass to prevent overfitting. Batch normalization layers compute batch statistics from the current mini-batch input and update their running mean and running variance estimates. Calling model.train() propagates the training mode to all submodules recursively. The default mode when a module is created is training mode. The counterpart is model.eval(), which switches to evaluation mode. Note that model.train() only changes module behavior for layers like dropout and batch normalization. It does not affect autograd or gradient tracking. To disable gradient computation, use torch.no_grad() separately. In a typical training loop, call model.train() before the training phase and model.eval() before the validation phase.

title: Module.eval
text: Module.eval() sets the module to evaluation mode. This is equivalent to calling model.train(False). In evaluation mode, layers with mode-dependent behavior change accordingly: dropout layers are disabled and pass all inputs through without zeroing any elements, and batch normalization layers use their stored running statistics (running mean and running variance) instead of computing statistics from the current batch. Calling model.eval() propagates the evaluation mode to all submodules recursively. It is important to understand that model.eval() only changes module behavior for layers like dropout and batch normalization. It does not disable gradient tracking or reduce memory usage. To disable gradient computation during inference, use torch.no_grad() as a separate step. The typical inference pattern is to call both model.eval() and wrap the forward pass in a torch.no_grad() context. model.eval() changes module behavior; torch.no_grad() controls gradient tracking. They serve different purposes and should both be used during evaluation.

title: nn.Dropout
text: torch.nn.Dropout(p=0.5, inplace=False) randomly zeroes some elements of the input tensor with probability p during training, using samples from a Bernoulli distribution. Each element is independently zeroed with probability p on every forward call. The remaining elements are scaled by 1/(1-p) to maintain the expected value of the output. This regularization technique helps prevent overfitting by forcing the network to not rely too heavily on any single neuron. During evaluation mode, dropout is completely disabled: all input elements are passed through unchanged with no scaling applied. The behavior is controlled by the module's training flag, which is set by model.train() and model.eval(). When the module is in training mode (model.train()), dropout is active. When in evaluation mode (model.eval()), dropout is disabled. This is why calling model.eval() before inference is important: if you forget, dropout will still randomly zero elements during prediction, producing inconsistent and degraded results. The inplace parameter controls whether the operation modifies the input tensor directly or creates a new tensor.

title: nn.BatchNorm2d
text: torch.nn.BatchNorm2d normalizes the input over the channel dimension using batch statistics during training and running statistics during evaluation. In training mode (model.train()), each forward pass computes the mean and variance from the current mini-batch, uses them to normalize the input, and updates the stored running mean and running variance using exponential moving average controlled by the momentum parameter. In evaluation mode (model.eval()), batch normalization uses the previously accumulated running statistics instead of computing statistics from the current input. This means the output is deterministic and does not depend on other samples in the batch. The running statistics are stored as running_mean and running_var buffers in the module. If the model is evaluated before sufficient training iterations, the running statistics may be inaccurate, leading to poor inference results. This is a common source of bugs: forgetting to call model.train() during training means running statistics are never updated, and the model will use incorrect statistics during evaluation. Similarly, forgetting model.eval() during inference means batch normalization will use mini-batch statistics, producing results that vary with batch composition. The key point is that batch normalization behaves differently in train mode and eval mode because of running statistics.

title: torch.no_grad
text: torch.no_grad() is a context manager and decorator that disables gradient tracking for all operations inside its scope. Operations performed inside a no_grad block are not recorded by autograd, so no gradient computation graph is built for them. Computation results inside the block will generally not track gradients. This reduces memory consumption and speeds up computation during inference, because PyTorch does not need to store intermediate results for backpropagation. Note that no_grad does not change the requires_grad attribute of existing tensors that were created outside the block; it only prevents new operations from being tracked. The typical usage is: with torch.no_grad(): output = model(input). torch.no_grad() is fundamentally different from model.eval(). model.eval() changes module behavior: it disables dropout and switches batch normalization to use running statistics. torch.no_grad() controls gradient tracking: it prevents autograd from recording operations. They address different concerns and are not interchangeable. During inference, you typically want both: model.eval() to get correct module behavior, and torch.no_grad() to save memory and computation. During training, you want model.train() and do not use torch.no_grad(), because gradients are needed for backpropagation. A common mistake is assuming that model.eval() also disables gradients. It does not. Another common mistake is assuming that torch.no_grad() changes dropout or batch normalization behavior. It does not. Each one controls a separate axis: model.eval() controls module behavior, torch.no_grad() controls gradient tracking.
